blocks.text: setTimeout runs a function after a delay in milliseconds. The rest of the program does not pause and wait — it keeps running. The delayed function is called later, on its own.

blocks.desc: Run fn after 2000 milliseconds — 2 seconds from now

lines: // Output order: A, C, then B one second later
// The program does NOT pause at setTimeout

blocks.label: The program does not pause
blocks.text: This surprises almost every beginner. Code after setTimeout runs immediately — before the delayed function fires. Think of it as scheduling a callback, not pausing execution.

exercises.title: Predict the order
tasks: Read the starter code without running it.
Write down the exact order you expect the letters to appear in the console.
Run it and compare to your prediction.
Now change the delay to 0ms. Does B appear before or after C? Why?
exercises.hint: setTimeout with 0ms delay still runs after the current code finishes — it is never truly immediate.

exercises.title: Comparing arrays
tasks: Create two arrays with the same numbers in different orders.
Try comparing them with === and log the result. Explain why it returns false.
Now sort both arrays and compare them as strings using .toString().
Confirm it returns true when contents match and false when they differ.
exercises.hint: Use .slice() before .sort() to avoid sorting the original array in place.
